title: The Battle of Thermopylae
status: assets generated on Higgsfield (2026-06-14); not yet assembled into final video

narration_script.notes: Two cold-open takes exist (draft + final). Final (later timestamp, mentions 'This is Empire Decoded') should be used as scene 1.

lines.text: What does it take to stop an empire of a million men? In four hundred and eighty B.C., three hundred Spartans found out — and for three days, they nearly did the impossible. This is Empire Decoded. This is the Battle of Thermopylae.

lines.role: cold_open_draft (alternate, not used if final exists)
lines.text: Three hundred men. One narrow pass. And an empire of more than a million soldiers that believed it could erase them from history. This is the story of the three days that changed the world — the Battle of Thermopylae.

In Sparta, a boy did not belong to his family. At age seven, he was taken from his home and placed into the agoge — a brutal state training system designed to produce one thing, and one thing only: the perfect soldier. Cold, hunger, and pain were not punishments. They were the curriculum.

Every Spartan warrior, or hoplite, fought the same way: locked shoulder to shoulder, shields overlapping, spears forward. This formation was called the phalanx — and for two hundred years, it made Sparta's army the most feared infantry force in the known world.

Before the war, Sparta's leaders traveled to the Oracle of Delphi for a prophecy. The answer they received was grim: either the city of Sparta would be destroyed — or one of her two kings would have to die so that it would not be. King Leonidas chose to be that king.

King Xerxes of Persia assembled the largest invasion force the ancient world had ever seen — by some ancient accounts, over a million soldiers, drawn from dozens of nations across his empire. His engineers built a bridge of boats across the Hellespont so the army could walk from Asia into Europe.

At the heart of that army marched the Immortals — ten thousand of Persia's finest soldiers, hand-picked, richly equipped, and undefeated in living memory. To the rest of the ancient world, they were less an army than a force of nature.

Leonidas chose his ground carefully: a narrow mountain pass at Thermopylae, barely wide enough for a handful of men to stand abreast. Here, the size of the Persian army meant nothing. For two days, three hundred Spartans and a few thousand allied Greeks held the line against wave after wave of attacks — in the rain, in the heat, without retreat.

On the third day, a local shepherd showed the Persians a hidden mountain path that led behind the Greek position. Surrounded on all sides, Leonidas sent the bulk of the Greek army away to fight another day — and stayed at Thermopylae with his three hundred Spartans to hold the line to the very last man.

They did not survive. But their stand bought Greece something it could not buy any other way: time. Time to gather its fleets, time to unite its city-states, and time to prepare for the battles that would ultimately turn the war — at Salamis, and at Plataea.

Twenty-five hundred years later, the names Sparta and Thermopylae still mean the same thing: standing your ground against impossible odds. The Greek world that survived that war went on to give civilization democracy, philosophy, and the foundations of Western thought. Subscribe to Empire Decoded — because history's greatest stories are still being decoded.
